verses.text: Ahab covets Naboth's vineyard, but Naboth refuses to sell his fathers' inheritance, which the law forbade him to give up. The king sulks like a child, refusing to eat. A heart that must have what it cannot rightly have turns its very abundance into misery.

Ahab covets Naboth's vineyard, but Naboth refuses to sell his fathers' inheritance, which the law forbade him to give up. The king sulks like a child, refusing to eat. A heart that must have what it cannot rightly have turns its very abundance into misery.

Jezebel scorns his weakness and promises to get the vineyard herself: 'dost thou now govern Israel?' She will solve by crime what he could not get by right. An evil partner emboldens sin, supplying the ruthlessness a weaker conscience lacked.

Jezebel scorns his weakness and promises to get the vineyard herself: 'dost thou now govern Israel?' She will solve by crime what he could not get by right. An evil partner emboldens sin, supplying the ruthlessness a weaker conscience lacked.

Jezebel scorns his weakness and promises to get the vineyard herself: 'dost thou now govern Israel?' She will solve by crime what he could not get by right. An evil partner emboldens sin, supplying the ruthlessness a weaker conscience lacked.

Jezebel writes letters in Ahab's name, arranging a fast and false witnesses to accuse Naboth of blaspheming God and the king. She cloaks murder in religion and law. The most monstrous crimes are often dressed in the forms of piety and justice.

Jezebel writes letters in Ahab's name, arranging a fast and false witnesses to accuse Naboth of blaspheming God and the king. She cloaks murder in religion and law. The most monstrous crimes are often dressed in the forms of piety and justice.

Jezebel writes letters in Ahab's name, arranging a fast and false witnesses to accuse Naboth of blaspheming God and the king. She cloaks murder in religion and law. The most monstrous crimes are often dressed in the forms of piety and justice.

The elders obey, the hired liars testify, and Naboth is stoned to death. A whole city bends to a queen's wicked command. When leaders are corrupt, ordinary men too easily become accomplices, choosing safety over an innocent life.

The elders obey, the hired liars testify, and Naboth is stoned to death. A whole city bends to a queen's wicked command. When leaders are corrupt, ordinary men too easily become accomplices, choosing safety over an innocent life.

The elders obey, the hired liars testify, and Naboth is stoned to death. A whole city bends to a queen's wicked command. When leaders are corrupt, ordinary men too easily become accomplices, choosing safety over an innocent life.

Jezebel tells Ahab Naboth is dead, and he rises at once to take possession of the vineyard. He asks no questions; the prize silences his conscience. Sin we did not commit ourselves still stains us when we gladly enjoy its fruit.

Jezebel tells Ahab Naboth is dead, and he rises at once to take possession of the vineyard. He asks no questions; the prize silences his conscience. Sin we did not commit ourselves still stains us when we gladly enjoy its fruit.

God sends Elijah to meet Ahab in the very vineyard: 'Hast thou killed, and also taken possession?' Where dogs licked Naboth's blood, they shall lick Ahab's. No crime is hidden from God; the stolen vineyard becomes the place of the murderer's sentence.

God sends Elijah to meet Ahab in the very vineyard: 'Hast thou killed, and also taken possession?' Where dogs licked Naboth's blood, they shall lick Ahab's. No crime is hidden from God; the stolen vineyard becomes the place of the murderer's sentence.

God sends Elijah to meet Ahab in the very vineyard: 'Hast thou killed, and also taken possession?' Where dogs licked Naboth's blood, they shall lick Ahab's. No crime is hidden from God; the stolen vineyard becomes the place of the murderer's sentence.

Ahab calls Elijah his enemy, but the prophet pronounces doom: his whole house will be cut off, and dogs will eat Jezebel by the wall of Jezreel. The reckoning is total. Those who sell themselves to do evil, as Ahab did, store up a fearful harvest.

Scripture sums him up: none sold himself to do evil like Ahab, whom Jezebel stirred up, following idols utterly. He is the very picture of a man given over to wickedness. A weak will yoked to a wicked counsellor sinks to the depths of sin.

Scripture sums him up: none sold himself to do evil like Ahab, whom Jezebel stirred up, following idols utterly. He is the very picture of a man given over to wickedness. A weak will yoked to a wicked counsellor sinks to the depths of sin.

Yet when Ahab hears the sentence, he tears his clothes, fasts, and goes softly — and God, seeing it, defers the judgment to his son's days. Even a faint, imperfect humbling moves God to mercy. If He so spares a man like Ahab, how ready He is to forgive the truly penitent.
